fields.description:
## Context

When any DSO workflow — brainstorm, fix-bug, implementation-plan, debugging, or user queries — needs to explore the codebase or web, it tasks a single agent with a broad question. That agent covers too much ground in one pass, producing misses, hallucinations, and shallow results. These bad results then inform key decisions (epic specs, task plans, bug hypotheses), compounding the damage downstream. Research on question decomposition for RAG shows +36.7% retrieval accuracy when complex questions are split into single-facet sub-questions before searching.

## Success Criteria

- A shared exploration decomposition protocol exists at plugins/dso/skills/shared/prompts/exploration-decomposition.md that any skill can read and follow
- The protocol classifies questions as SINGLE_SOURCE or MULTI_SOURCE using these rules: only scope to the codebase when a specific artifact is named; web is not a single source — broad web questions decompose by knowledge facet; codebase searches spanning multiple architectural layers are MULTI_SOURCE; ambiguity drives decomposition; preserve acronyms and unfamiliar terms without rephrasing
- MULTI_SOURCE questions are decomposed into scope-bounded sub-questions, each with a target scope and concrete deliverable
- Exploration agents include an escape hatch: if findings reveal the correct answer depends on a factor not specified in the question, or if two findings directly contradict each other, the agent returns DECOMPOSE_RECOMMENDED with proposed sub-questions instead of exhausting its context
- Re-decomposition is bounded to 1 level — a DECOMPOSE_RECOMMENDED triggers one re-dispatch round, not recursive decomposition
- Brainstorm, fix-bug, and implementation-plan are verified to follow the protocol — skill edits only if needed to achieve compliance

## Dependencies

None

## Approach

Shared decomposition prompt + structured aggregation protocol. A shared prompt at plugins/dso/skills/shared/prompts/exploration-decomposition.md defines classification rules, decomposition format, and an escape hatch for agents that discover hidden complexity during execution. Skills reference it the same way they reference prior-art-search.md. Verification tests confirm brainstorm, fix-bug, and implementation-plan reference the protocol.

## Research Findings

- Question decomposition for RAG (ACL 2025): +36.7% MRR and +11.6% F1 over standard RAG baselines when complex questions are split into single-hop sub-questions
- OpenAI Codex subagents: split analysis into smaller problems, return distilled takeaways to main thread
- GitHub Copilot /fleet: orchestrator breaks objectives into independent work items, dispatches multiple agents with one focused objective and a concrete deliverable each
- LangChain decomposition prompt: "break it down into distinct sub questions that you need to answer in order to answer the original question"
- Hierarchical Question Decomposition (umarbutler gist): JSON tree decomposition where sub-questions nest under parent questions with scope constraints
- Classification prompt v3 tested against 12 questions with iterative refinement of rules for codebase bias, web facet decomposition, ambiguity-driven decomposition, and pronoun scoping
